CURRENT AFFAIRS.

The old Gas Committee of charming, *“ tame " Clon-
mel haye exploded, and the town rumbles with oratory.
There were five members of the self-exploded commit-

~ tee, but at the special meeting called to ratify the ex-
plosion and elect a new committee only one turned up—
the Mayor. The Mayor, Mr. Meehan, is a tailor by
trade, and why he should have to stand amongst the
. debris whilst all the others had fled is more than we
can explain. There is an old libel that a tailor is only
‘the ninth part of a man, but here we have a tailor stand-
ing “to his fight whilst the other four members of the
e, who are non-tailors, Messrs. T. P.
0’Connor, T. Skehan, and J. Cahill, ap-
ought the place too hot for them, so they

Our readers may recollect that the chairman of the-
exploded Gas Committee, Alderman Morrissey, stated,
in connection with the selection of an Englishman—
the selection that generated the explosion—** they were-
completely in the hands of their consulting engineer.””
In other words, according to the chairman, the com--
mifttee were composed of so many eyphers, and it was
their English consulting engineer who had the appoint--
ing of a manager, as ‘‘ in these matters they were com-
pletely in the hands of their consulting engineer.”” We
musb not let the late Gas Committee wriggle out of that:
corner.

Mr. Mochan, the only one to stand on the burning*
deck, now attempts to take up the position that the
committee were only too anxious to recommend an Irish
candidate, but when it came to investigate the merits
of the candidates, the Irish candidates ‘‘ were hope-
lessly out of it with those from across the water.”” Buf
surely all that is as intangible as gas itself in view of the
previous statement of the chairman that: “ In these
matters they were completely in the hands of their con-
sulting engineer.”

There are always many comic clements in.these
municipal cases:  Councillor Roche is an undecided
man for he did not vote on the first occasion at all, and,
as we understand that he is an ex-soldier, one would
expect decision from him. At the last meeting he
asked if the committee had not picked out the best
man and added: ““ I would have the best man.”” Very
naturally a fellow-councillor asked: ““ Why didn’t you.
vote for him then? ”  And all the undecided councillor
could say was: ‘‘I didn’t vote at all,”” which remark
conveyed no new information to the Council. *“ Because.
you hadn’t the courage of your convictions ’ was the
retort of Councillor O'Meara.

The old committee having exploded as a result of a:
sort of spontaneous combustion, the following new
committee were elected :—Messrs. D. F. O’Connor, D.
['. O’'Meara, M. Tobin, P. J. Condon, and E. Burk
The new committee men were elected almost unani-
mously; there was one dissentient, the Mayor! Why.
the Mayor voted against the new committee is a
problem too deep for us, and we need not try to solve it..

Now this question of gas industry in Ireland is an
important matter. We have no sympathy with ap-
pointing a man to anything because he is an Irishman
if he is not efficient—nothing but certain disaster could
result from such a policy. We are glad this Clonmel
case has arisen, for it helps to draw attention to the.
state of Ireland in reference to the gas industry. So.
far ag we know the Department during all its ten years
as done nothing in this matter. ~We are not aware.
that it even ever considered it, and surely il is as much.
its business to do something to help the production
of Irishmen for the gas industry as to help Irishmen.
for the woollen industry. We suppose the Viceregal.
Mr. Tletcher is the official chiefly responsible with re-
gard to this side of the Department’s work, and if he-
thought more of Treland’s interests and less of running:
after Viceregal tea parties and things of that sort, it
would be better for the country.

It is plain that, notwithstanding all the years that
the gas industry has been established in Ireland, it has-
not been working in a way conducive to the industry:
falling naturally into the hands of Irish gas engineers.

— PR o e

in Ir,e.land‘, for we understand—though we have no
exact information on the point—that most even of the
Irish-born managers are of non-Irish extraction. Even
in Clonmel the local man, who many-consider is fully
competent to succeed his father as gas manager, is the
‘grandson of an importation from Scotland.

We think the Department ought to have tackled this
questign long hefore now, and we think the various
committees who have from time to time to elect gas
‘managars ought to have called for some scheme calcu-
lated to give a chance far the training of Irishmen for the
higher positions in the industyy if they found difficul-
ties.in finding qualified Irish candidates.

At the last meeting of the Clonmel Asylum Com-
mittee an application was made by the Hibernian In-
surgnee Co. for the insurance of the buildings. The
chairman remarked that the Hibernian was no more
Trish than Cardiff coals. That is a very extraordinary
remark to be made at this time of day, and it is utterly
incorrect. On & vote being taken as to whether or not
they would hear the agent of the company who was
present, a majority decided not to hear the agent.
Massrs. MeQuisk, Cumming and Morrissey voted in
favour of hearing him, but the chairman (Archdeacon
Flavin, P.P.), Dr. O’'Ryan, Mr. H. P. Ryan, and M.
Slattery voted against hearing him. What is up with
Clonmel ? The Hibernian is the only Irish fire insur-
ance that we have, yet a public committee in Clonmel
would not hear the representative of it. The chairman
said that the Hibernian had to depend on other com-
panies. If this means that the Hibernian, like all
other companies, distributes part of some of its risks
with other companies, it is true: and it could not carry
on its business on: any other basis. If this is to be a
reason for denying it business, then Ireland, once it
let, its insurance be done by anothex country, can never
do it for itself, unless not one but a whole series of
Irish companies that might between them keep all the
risks amongst themselves were established at once.
If this were the attitude adopted, Ireland could never
develop its own life insurance business, for an all-em-
bracing Irish fire insurance industry must grow by
degrees;; it cannot spring into existence in one moment.
The first insurance company must of necessity distri-
bute part of some of its risks, so that if such distri-
bution is fatal to its receiving Trish support, well then
there would be absolutely no possibility of ever estab-
lishing the fire insurance industry in Ireland! Fortu-
nately many Irish people and a large number of Trish
public bodies ‘do not take up. this absurd .and Irish
industry-stifling attitude.

The Technical Committee is another of the institu-
tions in charming ‘‘ tame’’ Clonmel that is worthy
of some comment. The head master of this
school i3 an Englishman; the building trades
teacher is an Englisiman with a salary of £140 a year;
the art master is an Englishman; the commercial
teacher is an Englishman. The teacher of the road car-
riage building class is a local man, fully qualified, and'
his salary.is £40 a year. In the new scheme for next
gession it is recommended thab the carriage building
clags be dropped,, and also it is suggested to dvop the
lace schopl. A local lady teaches the lace class ab a
salary of £52 a year, and’ there are about 50 girls: em-
ployed' in it. Tt is also recommended to get another
feacher, for the- engineering class.at £120' a year—an-

What are the precise terms of the Orange oath? In
a recent number of the ‘‘ Waterford News ’ there is a
version of it taken from a book published about a cen-
tury ago. Here it is:—

“1, A.B., do hereby swear that I will be true
to the King and Government, and that I will ex-
terminate, as far as I am able, the Catholics of
Ireland.”

We understand that some Waterford Orangemien say
that it is false, but they did not make the. assertion in
print or in writing.

A Gaelic League entertainment without as much as
one song in Irish is happily unique so far as we know.
It remained for Bagnalstown to make this record.
There was ‘‘ a grand Gaelic League dramatic enter-
tainment. and concert’’ in Bagnalstown on Monday
and also on Tuesdday of last week. It included ‘‘ two
side-splitting farces by the Gaelic League Dramatic
Class.” By the programme we see that one of the
dramatic items is called ‘‘ comic sketch,” and the
characters are Barney, Biddy, and My. Bullfrog; the
other is called ** Pat Molloy, the literary genius.”” Not
only is there not a song' in Trish on the programme but
there is not any familiax Anglo-Irish song.. There is a
song called *“ Homeland,” which is concerned with
this country, and a song-called “ Summer in Ireland.”’
A recitation is entitled ‘“ How Pat got to Heaven.”
An alleged comic song is entitled ‘“ Quarter to Two,””
possibly some *‘ comic™ about a drunkard coming home
at that hour: we don’t know for certain, however, as we
never heard the lyric. Another song on this pro-
gramme arranged by the Gaelic League of Bagnalstown

is called ‘““The Coster’s Dream,” and a Mr. W. P.

FEBRUARY 24, 1912.

~ We have received the following letter:—' Sir,—I
was very glad to see the fearless letter from the.Rev.
Dudley Fletcher. Will -you permit me to-add to his
instances of Rome’s interferences with the liberty of

the subject, ‘its opposition to Divorce, thus morally pre-

venting Roman Catholics from taking advantage of the
daws passed by an enlightoned T.ogislature.  Protes-
!;uu(is have nof hitherto advanced this instance of Papal
intolerance ; why, I.don’t know, as it is on all fours with
the rest.—I have, sir, the honour to be, your humble
and obedient servant, Canvin Serverus Kyox.”

We thought the Gaelic League concert in Bagnalstown
without an Irish song was the limit, but it is beaten by
the Chvistian Brothers’ Past Pupils’ Union being “‘non-
sectarian.”  Non-sectarian in the sense in which it is
wsed in this country means non-Catholie, and a Chris-
tian Brothers’ Union that is non-Catholic is.enough to
startle anyone in Ireland. The President of this non-
seetarian Christian Brothers’ Union is the Trinity Col-
lege Catholic, Serjeant T. F. Moloney, the eminent,
but yet unsuccessful, placehunter. What are the
views of this Trinity College Catholic and President of
the non-sectarian Christian Brothers’ Past Pupils’
Union on some general aspects of the education ques-
tion in these countries? This man contested an Eng-
lish eonstituency as a British Liberal, and he, there-

v be called a would-be British Liberal M.P.
What are the views of the English Liberals on educa-
tion, and is a would-be member of the British Liberal
a fit and proper person to be president of the

an Brothers’ Past Pupils’ Union?

In view of the extraordinary fact that this Union is
non-sectarian, perhaps, he is not so much out of place.
Mr. Keary Cahill called attention to the *‘ non-sec-
tarian ' question at the annual meeting, and Mr.
Patrick K. Cahill proposed that it be a Union of Catholic
Past Students. This proposal was defeated, so that
the Union remains non-sectarian—more “‘ tolerance
proving.”’ The Trinity College Catholic chairman re-
marked that the Christian Brothers had taught in Ire-
land for 110 years, and they did not reject a boy because
he knelt at a different altar. But surely that is no
parallel to making the Union non-sectarian. ~ The fact
that non-Catholic boys are free to go, and do go, to the
Christian Brothers for their schooling has not made the
Christian Brothers non-sectarian—the presence of non-
Catholic students in a Christian Brothers' School does
not mean that the Brothers give non-sectarian educa-
tion to their boys; the schools are Catholic s cheols ; and,
as we understand it, the ‘“ atmosphere " is not altered
to suit non-Cathol but the non-Catholics can go out
when religious exercises and instruction are being car-
ried on. That is ot ‘“ non-sectarianism.”’ Tt is full-
blooded Catholicity—non-Catholics persist in calling
Catholicity a !‘ sect’’—with perfect liberty to non-
Catholic boys to come for education and stay away from
the religious instruction. Tf the Union werc a Chris-
tian Brothers’ Past Students’ Union, with no mention

‘The 'Contractors

THE LEADER

of its being mon,sactarian, those non-Catholic past stu-
«dents who liked .could join it, and .if the Union had
.oecasion ito act as:a distinctly Catholic body, the non-
Catholics, if.any, could stay away. Such a.state would
ibe similar to what we understand exists with regard to
the schools.  Though .non-Catholics may and do take
advantage of the schools, the schools are before the
country as Catholic schools, and dare not ‘‘ non-sec-
tavian,”” which, as it ds understood in this.country,
meang non-Catholic.  The President, the Trinity Col-
lege Catholic and Placehunter, Serjeans Moloney, as &
would-be English Liberal M.P., takes, we assume, the
view of education that is held by the Liberal Party.
This matter mayseem to some a comparatively small
one, as the overwhelming body of members are sure to
be Catholic; but we think it is a very important point.
This turning of everything possible into ‘‘ non-see-
tarianism,”” particularly in this tame and tolerance
proving country, is wery enervating.

These appear to be days of great anti-Catholic
bigotry, and we hear that recruiting for the pernicious
society of the Freemasons is going on at extra
quick rate. Bigotry in the Post Office in Ireland is an
old story. About four yeavs ago—to be precise in
January, 1908—the Dublin Stores Branch of the Post
Office was withdrawn from the control of the Secretary
in Dublin and placed under the Controller of Stores
in London. We do not know if old Egerton made even
a protest, but we are quite sure that the old tame did
not make a fight.  Until that change had taken place
the tavo principal positions in the Dublin Stores Braneh
were held by Catholics ; three of the four principal posi-
tions are now held by non-Catholics. Five vacancies
for third class storemen (scale 22s. to 36s. a week) have
occurred since 1908; two of these have been filled by
the promotion of non-Catholics; three vacancies are still
open, and, as all the eligible candidates are ‘‘idola-
ters,”” it is proposed to fill them by dumping men from
England!  No doubt the dumps will be ‘‘ saved.”
Has the Irish Party nothing to say to this? Now that
a few Papists have got some positions under the Insur-
anee Act, one would almost think that the ‘‘saved”’’
were being strangled in their beds, so great is the out-
cry; but the anti-Catholic bigotry goes on, and we are
too tame to make any vigorous protest.

As we stated last week the Dublin Stores Branch is
now degraded to the rank of a sub-office under Bir-
mingham. As the Dublin Stores Braneh cantrols the
supply of stores to all Trish Post Offices, this means
that Ireland is treated as a subordinate Trish province
of an English city! Ireland is subordinate to Birming-
ham, and Ireland’s capital is degraded to the rank of a
Birmingham suburb! What has the Dublin Corpora-
tion to say to that? Trom headquarters in London—
St. Martin’s le Grand—an official contradiction has
been sent through Egerton to the statement in the Press
that Dublin has been subordinated to Birmingham.
But every Post Office official knows that this official

-contradiction is false, and we challenge Mr. Samuel,
"M.P., to deny on his word of honour that the staff
‘officer in Birmingham controls the Dublin Stores
Branch, -and that all important matters oceur-
ring at Dublin have to be submitted to the person at
Birmingham, who is shortly expected at Dublin to teach
the Dublin staff. His travelling and subsistence allow-
ances will come to something tidy, and what is he
coming for? No less than £1,300 has been squandered
on London officials who have come over here to teach
the staff how to do their work—to count the pens, we
.suppose, and perhaps weigh the coall Even the pro-
moted Protestants ave not trusted by the London offi-
wcials to do their worl correctly. The book-keeper to
‘the Accountant of the G.P.O. was selected to be Ac-
countant to the Irish Insurance Commissioners; buf
there is no one in the Accountant’s Offica, from the
Accountant downwards, who can be trusted by the
London officials to count the quires of paper in stock
in the Dublin Stores Branch.  There is decentralisa-
‘tion for you, Mr. Liberal and Home Rule Samuel,
"M.P.! Will the Irish Party tolerate the reduction of
‘Dublin to be a fief of Joe Chamberlain’s screw-making
stronghold?  Surely they have the power to make Mr.
Liberal Home Rule Samuel toe the line if they care
to exert it.  Captain Norton, the Assistant P.M.G.,
who also has a hand in this game, and attempts to
justify the sending over of Englishmen to fill vacancies
for which there are plenty competent men in Ireland,
is an alleged Irishman, and represents a London con-
stituency that has a large Irish vote. et him take
care that these tricks against Ireland do not cost hin
his seat at the next election. This same Captain
Norton, in his capacity as Chairman of the Factories
Committee, failed to recommend that Ireland should
get her fair share of Post Office expenditure by the
establishment of a factory in Dublin for repairing tele-
graph and telephone instruments. Such a factory
should find employment for from 50 to 100 men.

Under the heading ‘“ A Belfast Concert,”” a local
correspondent writes:—‘ T wonder have we anything
in Irish music or song worth presenting to an Irish
concert audience? A strange query atb this time of day
one might remark, but evidently the organiser or or-
ganisers of the ‘miscellaneous’ concert, whose pro-
-gramme I enclose, think we have not. You will note
the absence of anything Irish, or even Anglo-Irish, in
the list of items, and this is in a country where we are

-supposed to cherish national aspirations{ The strangest
_part of the business is that the concert was associated
with the most Irish and national part of Belfast. It
‘is evident that the Redemptorist Fathers, in whose
district it was organised, had nothing to do with the
character of the programme, for their Irish-Ireland
_sympathies are well known. - It is rather a pity, how-
ever, that some influence didn’t assert itself, and save
‘the audience (the vast majority of which would have
appreciated something reminding them that this was
TIreland) from having an exclusively non-Irish pro-
gramme thrust upon them. We are a patient and long-
-suffering people, and such ignoring of nationality could
only be possible in Ireland. And the pity of it, too,
that such first-class artistes should appear before an
Trish audience divorced, as it were, from the vast trea-
-sure-house of Irish song. It was disappointing and de-
nationalising to say the least of it; T had almost said
insulting.’” 5

We have received the following from a lady who
“signs herself for publication ‘ K.M.E."” * The first folio

of the letter is written on notepaper of the “_I}'ish
Women's Franchise League,’’ so that we suppose it is
a semi-official if not an official document:— s

“ Dear Sir,—In this week’s issue of your paper, in
tho column which deals with “ Current Affairs,” there
is o paragraph on Woman Suffrage, which, inasmuch
as it contains one or two mis-statements, I should be
glad of an opportunity to make the necessary correc-

“We are first told that ‘it will be time to consider

the question of ‘‘ Votes for Women * in Treland when

we get Home Rule.” In spite of all the unwavering
trust and implicit confidence which women place in the
superior wisdom of the other sox and in that sex's un-
swerving adherence to the time-honoured phrase,
‘ Ladies first,” it is rapidly becoming evident to them
that in the fascinating game of ‘ Party Politics* the
weakest invariably goes to the wall. Trish women are
beginning to realise, in a dim, vague way, that man in
his ordinary human capacitics is in most cases trust-
worthy, and, in some few, self-sacrifi ing, but that
when he steps into the arena of party politics he seems
automatically to put away from him ° all those things
which are of good report
forget they are dealing not with gentlemen, but with
politicians,” and so, remembering the fate of their South
African sisters, who left their enfranchisement to a

Home Rule Parliament, they insist that the time for

reform of any kind is always Now.

“The statement that ‘Irish women—real Irish
women—are not to any extent anxious to mix up in
active politics * would lead one to think that the women
of the Land League, the women ters of the '48
Movement, of the Siege of Limerick, and the Unionist
women of to-day are not real Irish women. >

““ We are further told that ‘ Ireland has not produced
one single woman leader capable by ability and force
of character of standing out as a ‘‘ Votes for Women "
champion, and that in order to make any sort of a splash
the imitation Suffragists in Ireland have to bring Miss
Pankhurst to Ireland.” Suffrage work in Ireland is con-
ducted on a more democratic basis than in England,
where the whole campaign rests on the shoulders of
wo or three women, who, as a natural consequence,
have to be in the limelight all the time, whereas all
the Trish Suffrage Societies carry on their work by
means of elected committees, so that no Irish woman

her. Tt is perhaps worthy of note that Miss Pankhurst’s
recent speech in Belfast has been the first delivered by
her in that city, although Belfast has a flourishing
Suffrage Society which has been in existence for over
three years, and that for every single meeting in Ire-
and which has been addressed by an Englishawomnn,
there have been hundreds held under the auspices of
Irish women only. When Inish women demand that o
! Votes for Women’ clause be inserted in the Home
’ulule Bill, it is not in any spirit of antagonism to that
Bill; to establish the franchise for Ireland on the basis
of the Local Government Register could not possibly,

ill.

phrase which describes Suffragists as the * sereeching
sisterhood.” Might I point out that we are morcé
nccusto_med to the epithet *shrieking.’ It is more
cuphonious, and if anything a trifle morg antediluvian.’’

We read a half-column report in the * Irish Times "
of the accounts given by the delegates sent by the:
Trish Women’s Franchise League to heckle Winston at
Belfast. So this League of imitation Sufiragettes:
thought well to add their little spiteful worst to.the.
situation that the Orange ruffians, led by Londonderry,
Carson and Co., had brought about. Miss Tatlow,
who presided, had no words to express her admiration
for those Nationalist women who went to a Nationalist
meebing—as a matter of fact, it was a Liberal meeting
and not a Nationalist one—and interrupted. We see by
the report that they had a man at their meeting, and he
read a paper—little Mr. Colum, the minor poet. Per-
haps, it is only in the fitness of things that nearly all
the men who are prominent Suffragettes, so far as we
know, strike us ag persons who really ought to dress-
in petticoats. One of the ‘“ Nationalist ’ delegates,
who gave an account of her ejection, was Mrs. Sheehy-
Skeflington—clarum et venerabile nomen! This lady
said Winston was a turncoat. There were six female
interrupters and one male, and of the six females five
wore Nationalists, and these facts dissipated, said Mrs.
Skeffington, the notion that they were playing the Tory
game. It appears to us to point the other way. Ac-
cording to the ‘‘ Irish Times’ '’ report, Mrs Sheehy-
Skeffington ended her speech as follow She was
responsible for interrupting Mr. Churchill’s famous
Tugela passage. (Laughter). The Suffragist heck-
ling was most effective, because it completely obscured:
the limelight which Mr. Churchill loved so much;.
they had managed to puncture the best parts-
of his speech. (Applause and laughter).”” So when,.
in the teeth of the blackguardism of Londonderry,
Carson and Co., a minister of a government committed -
to Home Rule, and about to bring in a Home Rule
Bill, addressed a meeting in Belfast, the so-called Irish-
Women'’s Franchise League greeted with applause and
laughter the statement of one of their delegates that

of his speech.”

We have received a copy of a double leaflet contain-
ing three hymns in Irish, which we can heartily re-
commend to all who wish to honour our National
Apostle, on his approaching Festival, with appropriate
hymns in the National language. The first is a trans-
lation into Irish by the late Mr. Thomas Flannery of &
hymn to St. Patrick, composed in Latin by St. Sech-
nall, who was one of the saint’s disciples. The fact
that the hymn dates back almost to St. Patrick’s own
time lends a special interest and charm to it. It is-
set to an ancient Irish ecclesiastical hymn-tune, which-
fits it to a nicety. The tune has an interest of its own,.
combining, as it does, a distinctly religious strain, with
the unmistakable characteristics of an Trish melody.
The second hymn is one to St. Brigid, our second
National Patron Saint, composed by the same M.
Flannery, and set to a very appropriate ecclesiastical
melody. The third is one to the Blessed Virgin—muipe
Mop—taken from the “ Religious Songs of Connaught,’”
set to an equally sweet melody. A new hymn to St.
Patrick is very welcome, ‘as we have been' limited so
long to one. A hymn to St. Brigid may deservedly find:
a place in the celebration of St. Patrick’s Day, as the
two saints were so intimately associated in the Chris-
tianising of Ireland, and their names have ever been:
entwined in the thoughts and affections of the Irish
people. It is a matter of reproach to us at the present
day that so little public honour is paid to St. Brigid,
the “ Mary of Brin,”’ whose Feast Day (February 1st):
passes almost unheeded by the mass of the people. This.

On' Monday night of this weel we received another
letter written on the notepaper of the ** Irish Women’s
Franchise League.” The.first letter and our comments
were already in type. The second letter is as fol-
lows :—** Sir,—Hitherto your cheap sneers at the en-
franchisement of women, having dealt in personalities
instead of argument, needed no reply. Last weel you
asked a question: ‘ What had Churchill, at the Belfast
meeting, to do with the English agitation for votes for
women? ' If the question is not purely rhetovical I
should like to give you the information for your'readers’
benefit.  Mr. Churchill was invited to speak in Ire-
land on the Home Rule Bill.  Irish women are in-
terested in the question of Home Rule. They would
like to know whether the people of Ireland are to' vote
for an Irish Parliament, or'only the male section of the
people? They wished to interrogate aresponsible Eng-
lish Cabinet Minister on the point. He refused to see a
deputation from two Irish Suffrage Societies. He also
ignored a wire, sent before the meeting, asking for en-
lightenment as to status of women in Home Rule Bill.
Not until a woman (an Irish Nationalist) had asked him
a direct question did he refer to a matter so vital to
Irish women. Five Irish Nationalists Lieckled Mr.
Churchill, and were subsequently ejected. Since then
Mr. Birrell has appointed a date to discuss this ques-
tion with Irish women. Cabinet Ministers (and even
editors) have been known to change their attitude to-
wards public questions when -challenged by public
opinion. Irish women will not allow the Liberal Gov-
ernment to shift its responsibilities in the matter of
their enfranchisement to the shoulders of a potential
Irish legislature.—HANNA SHEEHY-SKEFFINGTON.’”

CORRESPONDENCE.

CONCERNING IRISH GAS MANAGERS.

Dear Sir,—I have read your comments on the Clon-
mel gas squabble with interest. =~ With your remarks
in general I have no fault to find, but there is one pas-
sage which I must take exception to; it appears on
page 638, and is as follows:—"‘ There is a firm of gas
engineers in Westminster, England, that have a big
hold on portion of the gas industry in Ireland, and that
no doubt is another force making for the difficulty of
manning Irish gasworks with Irishmen.”” Now I can
safely assert that there is no individual or Irish town
council or corporation that has done more to further
the interests of Irishmen in the gas industry than this
“‘firm of gas engineersin Westminster” have done. They
have taken a keen interest in Irish talent, have paid
the fees of boys in their employment to attend techni-
cal classes, offered prizes for their efficiency in these
classes, given them every facility to learn and become
practically acquainted with all details in gas manufac-
ture, the technical, the manual and clerical branches,
and moreover, when opportunity offered, have taken
these deserving young Irishmen and placed them in
positions of trust in' their gasworks as managers, with
the result that at the present time there are seven
works under the care of this ‘‘ Westminster’’ firm,
manned by Irish-born, bred and trained men, all giv-
ing good results. Not only is this the case, but I know
of two instances where this “ Westminster ” firm have
talken young Irishmen and placed them in gas mana-
gers’ positions in England.  Knowing these facts, I
write this in order to dispel the erroneous impression
likely to be inferred from the. paragraph appearing as
above in your issue of 10th inst., and to give, in all
justice, credit where credit is due.—Yours, etc.,

I have said that the great majority of our fellow-
citizens will be directly affected by the working of the
Insurance Act, and this is evident from the fact thab
the following two big classes come into the ranks of
the compulsorily insured :— o

1. Hvery manual worker, no matter what- his in-
come, provided that he is working for an employer,
and is not his own master.

2. Tyery person over sixteen who is employed by
another, and whose rate of remuneration does not:
exceed £160 per annum.

It will be seen that, while the clerk whose salm"y
exceeds, even by a pound or two, £160 per annum, is
exempt, the skilled mechanic, because his employn?cnb s
is more precarious, is not exempt, though his earnings
should amount to twice as much. The case of such
casual workers as charwomen, ete., of commission
agents, of married women under certain conditions, of
employees entitled to pensions, sick pay, or other hene-
fits from their employers, arc separately dealt with in
the Act.

Having seen that the greater number of our citizens
come under one or other of the descriptions of compul-
sorily insured persons, the next point is to see how
they will be affected by the working of the Act. The
direct benefits that will accrue from it, if it works out
as well in practice as it promises in theory, are many,
while in various ways it is calculated to effect a con-
siderable improvement in the lives of the public in
general, of the uninsured as of the insured.

Undoubtedly the greatest dread that darkens the
worker’s horizon is the fear of falling ill, with its at-
tendant miseries of want and unemployment, when
he is a burthen instead of a support to those who should
be dependent on him. This haunting fear will be dis-
pelled by the working of the new Act. Every insured
worker will know that for 26 weeks in every year he
will be entitled to get 10s. a week in return for his
contributions—a woman gets 7s. 6d. a week in return
or her reduced contributions—whether these 26 weeks
represent one long illness or several short spells spread
over the year. Moreover, should his illness continue
onger than 26 weeks, or should he become disabled
in any way, and be unable to recover compensation
under the Workmen’s Compensation Act, or otherwise,
he will, after the first 26 weeks during which he has
heen in receipt of 10s. a week, and provided that he
has paid up his contributions for two years, become
entitled to a payment of 5s. a week till he recovers or
till death ends his sufferings. That this will remove a
great source of anxiety from the worker who can just
mangge to pay his way, with little or no chance of
saving for a rainy day, no one can deny.

The insured worker is certain, however, not only of
a weekly payment to keep things going while he, the
breadwinner, is unable to provide for his own needs
and those of his dependents, but he knows that should

amongst th with whom he is in hourly communi-

himself. This part of the Act ought to exercise a more

e check on the spread of consumption than any

While on the subject of sanatoria, it may be men-

#ioned that the Act provides a special grant from Par-
liament of 1% millions sterling for the building of sana-
toria throughout the United Kingdom, and it is from
this fund that cur wide-awake alderman, Dr. McWal-
ter, trying to recover for the Dublin Corporation
some of the money spent on the Brittas Sanatorium.

It should be noted that while an insured worker is

. in receipt of sick or disablement 'pay, or is in a sana-

The Insurance Act is not only solicitous for the
health of those who are of an age to earn, but it en-
deavours to give a good start in life to those who will
later on be the wage-earners. With this end in view,
it provides that a maternity benefit of 80s. in cash or

< mirable. He says that Mr. Dudley Fletcher’s
communication ‘‘ goes over a variety of things, inac-
curate in many things, and relevant in nothing ’’; he
does not enter into controversy with the rector of Cool-
banagher.

Why do I write this article? M. Fletcher says that
Protestants object to Home Rule because an Irish Par-
liament might be dominated by the Church of Rome;
that Ireland has had recently two examples of Rome
Rule—the Decree ‘“ Ne temere ” and the Motu Proprio
*“ Quantavis Diligentia ’; that no such difficulty would
arise from a Home Rule Church, such as the Reformed
Church in Ireland. I am not going into the merits or
demerits of the Decree ‘‘ Ne temere,”” or of the Motu
Proprio; but I say that Mr. Dudley Fletcher, accord-
ing to his own principles, should be one of the last men
in Ireland to raise his voice or talke up his pen against
one or the other. Again I am not writing a polemical
article on the Reformed Church in Ireland; but I say
very confidently that, judged by the fruits which that
Reformed Church has borne in Mr. Fletcher himself
and in many others of the reformed ministers in Tre-
land, it is not likely to have much attractiveness for
Romans.

‘“ Protestants,’” says Mr. Fletcher, ‘‘ are satisfied to
be governed from Westminster, because, on the whole,
they believe -that they get fair play there. . . . They
are not particularly enamoured with Westminster rule
as exhibited by the present regime.’”” = There is ques-
tion, bear in mind, of fair play to Protestants. Per-
sons may or may not object to the present regime on
general grounds affecting all classes. But why should
Trish Protestants, as Protestants, be dissatisfied with
the present regime? Does Mr. Fletcher want not
merely to be governed from Westwinster, but to be
governed by a Tory Government? Does he want coer-
cion to be revived? or the National University
abolished ? or the 0ld Age Pensions and Insurance Acts

“ revoked? or Protestant landlordism restored to its

former domination? One can understand why a mem-
ber of the Reformed Church should object to be gov-
erned from Rome; but what has the present regime
done to Irish Protestants as such that they should be
disgatisfied with it? 3

But to return to the Decree “ Ne temere "’ and the
recent Motu Proprio, and to the consideration of the
“Home Rule Church ” in Treland. I have said that

these Papal acts, Mr. Fletcher was one of the last who

with unctuous complacency, is free from atheism and
infidelity; but look at Italy where the footsteps of

atheists in Catholic countries have more Chrigtian faith

how does he differ in his beliefs, say, from those who
are called Freethinkers in France? .

Church.””

It all’s well with the ‘“ Home Rule Church,”” what
was the necessity of Bishop Bernard’s visit to St.
Petersburgh ? And about that visit one would like to
know : Did the Russian ecclesiastical authorities recog-
nise the validity of Dr. Bernard’s Orders? Did they
admit him to officiate in a Russian church? Did they
even admit him to the sacraments in their churches—
did he go to Confession or receive Holy Communion
in a Russian church? And is it not strange that a
gentleman who denounces Transubstantiation at
home should be coquetting with the Russian Church,
which teaches Transubstantiation. And if Protestant
ministers are afraid of Roman domination, is it not
strange that they should look to Russia as the ideal of
civil and religious freedom ? D.C.

MAYO COUNTY INFIRMARY.

Appointment of Matron.

The Joint Committee of Management of above In-
stitution will on Saturday, the 2nd day of March, 1912,
at the hour of 12 o’clock noon, proceed to appoint a
fully qualified and trained Nurse as Matron to the
County Infirmary, who shall, under the Medical Super-
intendent, take full charge of all the Nursing arrange-
ments, catering, and internal economy of the above
Institution, at a salary of £50 per annum, with rations,
furnished apartments, fire, light, attendance, ete.

Applicants must have a certificate of at least three
years’ training from a large general City Hospital
having a recognised Training School. She must have
at least six months’ experience in an Operating Theatre,
and have acted as ‘‘ Nurse in- Charge '’ or ‘‘ Night
Matron ”’ for at least six months in a large general
City Hospital.

Applicants must be between the ages of 27 and 40
years.

The appointment will be for six months on probation.
Confirmation of the appointment will be made at the
expiration of that period only on a satisfactory report
being received from the Resident Surgeon.

Three months’ notice in writing by either party fo
terminate the appointment.

The person appointed must enter into a Bond with
two solvent sureties in the sum of £200 for the due
performance of her duties.

Applications, stating age and accompanied by certi-
ficate of training, testimonials, and references, must
be lodged with me on or before 1st March, 1912.

HENRY R. SHERIDAN,
Secretary.

ST. CLARE'S ORPHANAGE, HAROLD’S CROSS.

Founded A.D. 1803.

The Rev. T. A. Fitzgerald, O.S.T., will make an Appeal in
favour of our Orphanage next Sunday, 25th February, in
the Church of our Lady of Refuge, Rathmines, after the
First Gospel of the last Mass. In the evening another
Appeal will be made for the same charitable object.

As the Anpual Charity' Sermon . is the only occasion on
which the Orphanage and its works are brought before the
Public, we respectfuly request your kind attendance.

This being the first Institution for Orphan girls founded
in Dublin, its claims on general support may be justly
urged, especially as the helpless little ones whosc_cg\usc
we plead are particularly unsuited to encounter the difficul-
ties and hardships which beset the young, innocent, and
unprotected.

Hundréds of friendless girls, many of whom were once
blessed with loving parents and happy homes, have sought
a safe asylum in the Orphanage, and therein have received
careful training in religious and domestic duties, which,
united with a solid practical education, prepared them to
fulfil the duties of their varied positions in life in a truly
Christian’ manner, and with' much edification.

The benefactors of the Orphanage are entreated to ¢on-
tribute with their usual generosity, to keep the Orphanage
in its present cfficient working order. Everything is done
for the comfort, as well as for the instruction of the children,
who are taught Typewriting, the use of Knitting and Sewing
s, Domestic Duties; also Laundry work and

The Orphans pray daily for their benefactors, for whom
also forty-cight Masses are offered yearly.

Donations from those who cannot aftend the Sermons
shall be gratefully received by all the Clergymen of the
Parish, and by the Reverend Preachers.
